skill.name: chipsec
skill.description: Analyze UEFI and BIOS firmware dumps with Intel CHIPSEC using offline, static workflows. The skill guides threat scans, EFI inventories, baseline comparisons, structure decoding, NVRAM extraction, and SPI descriptor review without live hardware access.
skill.summary: Guide offline UEFI and BIOS firmware analysis with CHIPSEC.
skill.author: BrownFineSecurity

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command, malware-keyword, weak-hash, and filesystem alerts, but most are Markdown examples for defensive offline firmware analysis. The confirmed concern is repeated guidance to run sudo chmod 777 on a Python site-packages logs directory, which is unsafe documentation for users. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, or malicious automation was found in the reviewed files.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe World-Writable System Directory Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to run sudo mkdir under Python site-packages and then sudo chmod 777 on the logs directory. This is not malicious, but it teaches a world-writable permission pattern in a privileged application directory, which can enable local tampering or unsafe shared-host behavior.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact chmod 777 recommendation appears in both user-facing instructions and the implementation plan. The risk is documentation-driven rather than executable malware, so medium severity is appropriate.

low_findings.title: Defensive Command Examples Flagged as Execution Risk
low_findings.description: The static scanner flagged many shell commands, pipes, command substitutions, grep, binwalk, file, diff, and CHIPSEC invocations. These appear inside Markdown code blocks for offline firmware analysis, use user-supplied firmware paths, and are not executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands are documentation examples with defensive CHIPSEC workflows. They still deserve user caution because users may copy commands into a shell.

low_findings.title: Malware and C2 Terms Used as Defensive Analysis Vocabulary
low_findings.description: Terms such as rootkit, malware, implant, blocked binary, and backdoor describe threats that CHIPSEC can detect. The reviewed context documents detection and incident response, not creation or deployment of malware.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is clearly defensive firmware scanning and known-threat identification. No evidence found of payload generation, persistence instructions, or command-and-control setup.

low_findings.title: Weak Hash Names Are Output Fields, Not Cryptographic Choices
low_findings.description: MD5 and SHA1 appear in sample CHIPSEC output and inventory descriptions alongside SHA256. The skill does not ask users to rely on weak hashes for trust decisions or implement cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The weak hash strings are presented as tool output metadata. The same examples include SHA256 for stronger identification.

dangerous_patterns.title: Privileged chmod 777 Setup Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation recommends sudo chmod 777 for a logs directory under site-packages. Marketplace users should be warned or the skill should replace this with a least-privilege directory ownership fix.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit, repeated, and affects a privileged filesystem location. The concern is unsafe operational guidance, not hidden execution.

content.value_statement: Firmware security reviews are difficult when teams only have offline BIOS or UEFI dump files. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured CHIPSEC workflow for threat scans, inventories, comparisons, and reporting.
actual_capabilities: Guides offline CHIPSEC scans for known UEFI threats and vulnerable modules.
Generates EFI executable inventory workflows with hashes for baseline comparison.
Explains how to decode firmware structure and review extracted volumes and files.
Shows how to locate NVRAM variables produced by static firmware decoding.
Supports before-and-after firmware update comparison workflows.
Documents common CHIPSEC errors and practical troubleshooting steps.
limitations: Requires CHIPSEC and supporting command-line tools to be installed separately.
Does not perform live hardware checks or kernel-driver-based CHIPSEC modules.
Cannot prove firmware is safe when blocklist scans return no matches.
Some examples recommend broad permissions and should be reviewed before use.

use_cases.title: Review a Vendor Firmware Image
use_cases.description: Run a repeatable offline workflow to scan a BIOS or UEFI image, produce an EFI inventory, and document findings.

use_cases.title: Compare Firmware Before and After an Update
use_cases.description: Create baselines from known images and compare updated firmware for changed modules, missing entries, or unexpected additions.

use_cases.title: Triage Suspected Firmware Compromise
use_cases.description: Scan a suspect dump for known UEFI threats, decode its structure, and collect hashes for deeper investigation.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Firmware Scan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner user with one firmware dump
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chipsec skill to plan a safe offline scan for firmware.bin. Include the main CHIPSEC commands, expected outputs, and what I should save.

prompt_templates.title: Build an EFI Inventory Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chipsec skill to create an EFI inventory workflow for my firmware image. Explain how to name the outputs and how to use the inventory later.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Two Firmware Images
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chipsec skill to compare original_firmware.bin and updated_firmware.bin. Focus on baseline generation, CHIPSEC checks, and findings I should report.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Incident Response Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the chipsec skill to design an incident response workflow for a suspect UEFI dump. Include threat scanning, decoding, NVRAM review, hash collection, and reporting priorities.

output_examples.input: Analyze a single vendor firmware dump for known UEFI threats.
output_examples.output: A concise workflow with a threat scan first, an EFI inventory second, firmware decoding third, and a short checklist for interpreting warnings.

output_examples.input: Compare a known-good firmware image with a suspect image.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step comparison plan that creates inventories, checks the suspect image against the baseline, and highlights changed modules for review.

output_examples.input: Prepare a firmware incident response summary.
output_examples.output: A report outline covering threat names, affected module identifiers, hashes, decoded artifacts, NVRAM notes, and remediation recommendations.

best_practices: Run analysis only on firmware you own or are authorized to review.
Keep original firmware dumps read-only and store generated outputs in a separate analysis directory.
Review privileged setup commands before running them and prefer least-privilege permissions.
anti_patterns: Do not run live CHIPSEC modules when the goal is offline dump analysis.
Do not treat a clean blocklist scan as proof that firmware is trustworthy.
Do not apply world-writable permissions to system directories without a security review.

faq.question: Does this skill require access to live hardware?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on offline analysis of firmware dump files and avoids kernel-driver-based CHIPSEC checks.

faq.question: Can it detect every firmware implant?
faq.answer: No. It helps find known threats and suspicious changes, but unknown implants require deeper reverse engineering.

faq.question: What firmware formats does it cover?
faq.answer: It documents workflows for common BIOS and UEFI dump formats such as BIN, ROM, FD, CAP, and firmware volumes.

faq.question: Why does the audit show a medium risk level?
faq.answer: The files include many command examples, and some setup guidance uses unsafe world-writable permissions in a system directory.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible skill workflows.

faq.question: Does it modify firmware images?
faq.answer: No. The documented workflows analyze, inventory, decode, and compare firmware dumps without writing changes to the firmware image.
